summary: Gruber examines the backlash from ChatGPT users who emotionally mourned the deprecation of older models like GPT-4o when OpenAI launched GPT-5. He draws a parallel to the movie Her, noting that some users formed genuine emotional attachments to specific AI models, treating them as friends or partners. While he finds the sycophantic tendencies of chatbots transparently phony, he acknowledges the legitimate complaint from power users who relied on specific models for distinct tasks. He argues that GPT-5's automatic model routing is actually progress for most users, and that expecting old cloud-based models to remain available indefinitely is unreasonable.
key_points: Some ChatGPT users formed deep emotional attachments to specific models, reacting to GPT-5's launch as a personal loss rather than a product change
Gruber finds LLM sycophancy transparently phony and is surprised how many people were genuinely fooled by it
GPT-5's automatic routing between sub-models is a product improvement for most users, even if power users who memorized model strengths see it as a step backward
Cloud computing fundamentally changes the relationship users have with software versions — you can't just keep running the old one
The scale of emotional AI attachment, even as a small percentage of 800 million users, raises concerns about how society will handle increasingly human-mimicking AI
money_quotes: That tendency toward cloying-ness and abject sycophancy ('Great question!') is exactly what I like least about LLM chatbots, including ChatGPT.
I'm unsurprised that some people like it, but I am a little taken aback by how many people seem to have been fooled by it. It's not just phony, but to me, transparently phony.
These people need help, and that help isn't going to come from a chatbot.
It gives me pause about how we, collectively, are going to react as AI gets better at mimicking human emotions, tone, and responses.
With each improvement, more people are convinced, wrongly, that there's some sort of sentience behind these things.
I couldn't be bothered to learn and remember which models were better for which tasks. Their inscrutable naming and numbering schemes made things seem deliberately confusing.
But for users who could be bothered to learn and remember which models were better for which tasks, it's obvious to see how this seems like a step backward. But that's progress.

When the industry revolved around software you installed on your computers, if a new version came out that you didn't like, you could just keep using the old version. That's not how 'cloud computing' works.
key_insight: The emotional backlash to GPT-5 replacing older models reveals a troubling new dimension of human-AI attachment, but the real product lesson is that cloud-based AI doesn't owe users perpetual access to deprecated models.
